digest.title: 3-month fluoride varnish recall outperforms 6-month for high-risk adult caries
digest.summary: Multi-center Indian trial shows 38% lower caries recurrence with 3-month vs 6-month recall in adults with active decay history. No effect in low-risk patients.
digest.actionable: Reassess recall interval for adults flagged high-risk in your charting

digest.title: DCI revised radiograph dose limits effective 2026-12-15
digest.summary: Maximum dose per IOPA exposure drops from 1.5 mSv to 1.0 mSv. E-speed film passes at the new limit; D-speed does not. Digital RVG sensors unaffected.
digest.actionable: Audit your X-ray setup before Dec 15; document E-speed or RVG in your SOPs

patient_content_library.title: 3 things your teeth tell you about your heart
patient_content_library.body: Periodontal disease shares inflammation pathways with cardiovascular disease. Bleeding gums, persistent bad breath, and loose teeth in middle age are early signals. A 2024 meta-analysis of 18 studies found periodontal patients had 22% higher CVD events. The simplest action: scaling every 6 months reduces both gum and inflammation markers.

patient_content_library.title: Why your child resists brushing — and what to try this week
patient_content_library.body: Most resistance under age 7 is sensory, not behavioural. Try: (1) a softer brush than you think necessary, (2) brushing in front of a mirror so they see what's happening, (3) two minutes of music as the timer. If resistance persists past 4 weeks, it's worth a pediatric dental consult.

patient_content_library.title: Aligners or braces? What I tell my patients in their 30s
patient_content_library.body: Aligners work for ~70% of cases — mild-to-moderate crowding, spacing, and minor rotations. Braces still win for severe rotations, deep bites, and skeletal corrections. The tell: if you can't keep aligners on for 22 hours/day, braces are the safer choice.

digest.title: Olaplex No.9 launches in India — bond protector for chemically-treated hair
digest.summary: Pre-shampoo bond protector targeting damage from frequent colour and smoothening. Distributors confirmed in Delhi/Mumbai/Bangalore. Wholesale ₹3,200/bottle, retail ~₹4,500.
digest.actionable: Worth piloting if your colour client mix is >25%

digest.title: Formaldehyde-free smoothening alternatives gaining share — citric-acid based
digest.summary: Brazilian citric-acid keratin alternatives now widely available. Lower fume, comparable hold for ~6-8 weeks. Pricing roughly +15% over standard keratin.
digest.actionable: Position as 'safe smoothening' for pregnancy / asthma clients

digest.title: Wedding season opener — first lean April-May window before main Oct-Dec rush
digest.summary: Mini-bridal window April-May (~15% of annual bridal volume) often missed by salons focused on Oct-Dec. Pre-wedding skincare + mehendi-prep services peak now.
digest.actionable: Run a 'Bridal Trial @ ₹999' offer; book 2-month skincare package

digest.title: L'Oreal Professionnel India training: Advanced Balayage Masterclass
digest.summary: 2-day in-person training in Mumbai. ₹15,000 fee. Targeted at stylists with 2+ years of colour experience.
digest.actionable: Worth subsidising for one stylist if balayage demand is rising in your locality

digest.title: 'Walk-in available' tag on GBP boosting calls 23% in metros
digest.summary: Salons that explicitly add 'walk-in available' to their GBP description see 23% higher call volume in the same locality vs salons without. Effect strongest weekday afternoons.
digest.actionable: Add the tag to your GBP description this week

patient_content_library.title: Is keratin safe? What to ask before booking
patient_content_library.body: Old-school keratin uses formaldehyde — safe in trained hands but not for pregnancy or asthma. Citric-acid alternatives exist now with similar hold. Two questions to ask any salon: (1) what's your activator? (2) is the room ventilated? If you don't get a clear answer, look elsewhere.

patient_content_library.title: Balayage vs highlights — which actually suits you?
patient_content_library.body: Highlights are uniform stripes; balayage is hand-painted, growing out softly with no visible regrowth line. Highlights cost less but need touch-ups every 8 weeks. Balayage holds 4-5 months. If you don't visit the salon often, balayage is the practical choice.

patient_content_library.title: Post-colour care — first 72 hours matter most
patient_content_library.body: Three rules: no shampoo for 48h (let cuticle close), use sulphate-free shampoo from day 3, and avoid hot water washes for the first month. Skip these and you'll lose 30% of the colour vibrance in the first wash.

patient_content_library.title: First 30 days at the gym — what actually changes
patient_content_library.body: Week 1-2: nervous-system adaptation, lifts feel easier (not muscle gain — coordination). Week 3-4: small visible changes, sleep improves. Real strength/aesthetic changes start at week 8-12. Most people quit at week 3 expecting visible change. Trust the process.

patient_content_library.title: PT or solo? A 5-question test
patient_content_library.body: Solo if: you've trained before, you can read program structure, you don't get injured easily. PT if: you're new, recovering from injury, training for a specific event, or have inconsistent form. Hybrid (PT 1-2x/month + solo): the smartest middle path for most members.

patient_content_library.title: How much protein do you actually need?
patient_content_library.body: Sedentary adults: 0.8g/kg body weight. Active adults: 1.2-1.6g/kg. Strength training: 1.6-2.2g/kg. For a 70kg active person, that's 84-112g/day — roughly 4 fistful-portions of protein. Indian-veg diets often fall short; supplement with paneer, dal, tofu before adding powders.

patient_content_library.title: Generic vs branded medicines — what's actually different?
patient_content_library.body: The active molecule is identical and regulator-tested. Difference is in the binders, fillers, and brand markup. Generic is typically 40-60% cheaper. Two cases where brand may help: (1) very narrow-therapeutic-index drugs (some thyroid, blood thinners) where bioequivalence varies, (2) extended-release formulations. Otherwise, generic is the rational choice.

patient_content_library.title: Where to NOT store your medicines
patient_content_library.body: Three places: (1) bathroom — humidity wrecks tablet integrity. (2) car glove box — temperature swings degrade most molecules in a month. (3) refrigerator door — temperature varies too much; use the main shelf if cold storage is needed. The kitchen at room temperature, away from sunlight, is best.

patient_content_library.title: Summer first-aid: what every Indian household should have
patient_content_library.body: Five items: ORS sachets (heat-stroke is sudden), broad-spectrum sunscreen SPF 50+ (apply 30 min before sun), anti-fungal cream (sweat triggers infections), Vaseline (chafing relief), and a thermometer. Skip energy drinks marketed as 'summer drinks' — sugar load is counterproductive when dehydrated.

patient_content_library.title: Generic vs branded medicines — what's actually different?
patient_content_library.body: The active molecule is identical and regulator-tested. Difference is in the binders, fillers, and brand markup. Generic is typically 40-60% cheaper. Two cases where brand may help: (1) very narrow-therapeutic-index drugs (some thyroid, blood thinners) where bioequivalence varies, (2) extended-release formulations. Otherwise, generic is the rational choice.

patient_content_library.title: Where to NOT store your medicines

patient_content_library.title: 3 things your teeth tell you about your heart
patient_content_library.body: Periodontal disease shares inflammation pathways with cardiovascular disease. Bleeding gums, persistent bad breath, and loose teeth in middle age are early signals. A 2024 meta-analysis of 18 studies found periodontal patients had 22% higher CVD events. The simplest action: scaling every 6 months reduces both gum and inflammation markers.

patient_content_library.title: Why your child resists brushing — and what to try this week
patient_content_library.body: Most resistance under age 7 is sensory, not behavioural. Try: (1) a softer brush than you think necessary, (2) brushing in front of a mirror so they see what's happening, (3) two minutes of music as the timer. If resistance persists past 4 weeks, it's worth a pediatric dental consult.

patient_content_library.title: Aligners or braces? What I tell my patients in their 30s
patient_content_library.body: Aligners work for ~70% of cases — mild-to-moderate crowding, spacing, and minor rotations. Braces still win for severe rotations, deep bites, and skeletal corrections. The tell: if you can't keep aligners on for 22 hours/day, braces are the safer choice.

patient_content_library.title: First 30 days at the gym — what actually changes
patient_content_library.body: Week 1-2: nervous-system adaptation, lifts feel easier (not muscle gain — coordination). Week 3-4: small visible changes, sleep improves. Real strength/aesthetic changes start at week 8-12. Most people quit at week 3 expecting visible change. Trust the process.

patient_content_library.title: PT or solo? A 5-question test
patient_content_library.body: Solo if: you've trained before, you can read program structure, you don't get injured easily. PT if: you're new, recovering from injury, training for a specific event, or have inconsistent form. Hybrid (PT 1-2x/month + solo): the smartest middle path for most members.

patient_content_library.title: How much protein do you actually need?
patient_content_library.body: Sedentary adults: 0.8g/kg body weight. Active adults: 1.2-1.6g/kg. Strength training: 1.6-2.2g/kg. For a 70kg active person, that's 84-112g/day — roughly 4 fistful-portions of protein. Indian-veg diets often fall short; supplement with paneer, dal, tofu before adding powders.

patient_content_library.title: Is keratin safe? What to ask before booking
patient_content_library.body: Old-school keratin uses formaldehyde — safe in trained hands but not for pregnancy or asthma. Citric-acid alternatives exist now with similar hold. Two questions to ask any salon: (1) what's your activator? (2) is the room ventilated? If you don't get a clear answer, look elsewhere.

patient_content_library.title: Balayage vs highlights — which actually suits you?
patient_content_library.body: Highlights are uniform stripes; balayage is hand-painted, growing out softly with no visible regrowth line. Highlights cost less but need touch-ups every 8 weeks. Balayage holds 4-5 months. If you don't visit the salon often, balayage is the practical choice.

patient_content_library.title: Post-colour care — first 72 hours matter most
patient_content_library.body: Three rules: no shampoo for 48h (let cuticle close), use sulphate-free shampoo from day 3, and avoid hot water washes for the first month. Skip these and you'll lose 30% of the colour vibrance in the first wash.

patient_content_library.body: Solo if: you've trained before, you can read program structure, you don't get injured easily. PT if: you're new, recovering from injury, training for a specific event, or have inconsistent form. Hybrid (PT 1-2x/month + solo): the smartest middle path for most members.

patient_content_library.title: How much protein do you actually need?
patient_content_library.body: Sedentary adults: 0.8g/kg body weight. Active adults: 1.2-1.6g/kg. Strength training: 1.6-2.2g/kg. For a 70kg active person, that's 84-112g/day — roughly 4 fistful-portions of protein. Indian-veg diets often fall short; supplement with paneer, dal, tofu before adding powders.

patient_content_library.title: Generic vs branded medicines — what's actually different?

patient_content_library.title: 3 things your teeth tell you about your heart
patient_content_library.body: Periodontal disease shares inflammation pathways with cardiovascular disease. Bleeding gums, persistent bad breath, and loose teeth in middle age are early signals. A 2024 meta-analysis of 18 studies found periodontal patients had 22% higher CVD events. The simplest action: scaling every 6 months reduces both gum and inflammation markers.

patient_content_library.title: Why your child resists brushing — and what to try this week
patient_content_library.body: Most resistance under age 7 is sensory, not behavioural. Try: (1) a softer brush than you think necessary, (2) brushing in front of a mirror so they see what's happening, (3) two minutes of music as the timer. If resistance persists past 4 weeks, it's worth a pediatric dental consult.

patient_content_library.title: Aligners or braces? What I tell my patients in their 30s
patient_content_library.body: Aligners work for ~70% of cases — mild-to-moderate crowding, spacing, and minor rotations. Braces still win for severe rotations, deep bites, and skeletal corrections. The tell: if you can't keep aligners on for 22 hours/day, braces are the safer choice.

patient_content_library.body: Five items: ORS sachets (heat-stroke is sudden), broad-spectrum sunscreen SPF 50+ (apply 30 min before sun), anti-fungal cream (sweat triggers infections), Vaseline (chafing relief), and a thermometer. Skip energy drinks marketed as 'summer drinks' — sugar load is counterproductive when dehydrated.

patient_content_library.title: Is keratin safe? What to ask before booking

patient_content_library.title: Balayage vs highlights — which actually suits you?
patient_content_library.body: Highlights are uniform stripes; balayage is hand-painted, growing out softly with no visible regrowth line. Highlights cost less but need touch-ups every 8 weeks. Balayage holds 4-5 months. If you don't visit the salon often, balayage is the practical choice.

patient_content_library.title: Post-colour care — first 72 hours matter most
patient_content_library.body: Three rules: no shampoo for 48h (let cuticle close), use sulphate-free shampoo from day 3, and avoid hot water washes for the first month. Skip these and you'll lose 30% of the colour vibrance in the first wash.

patient_content_library.title: 3 things your teeth tell you about your heart
patient_content_library.body: Periodontal disease shares inflammation pathways with cardiovascular disease. Bleeding gums, persistent bad breath, and loose teeth in middle age are early signals. A 2024 meta-analysis of 18 studies found periodontal patients had 22% higher CVD events. The simplest action: scaling every 6 months reduces both gum and inflammation markers.

patient_content_library.title: Why your child resists brushing — and what to try this week
patient_content_library.body: Most resistance under age 7 is sensory, not behavioural. Try: (1) a softer brush than you think necessary, (2) brushing in front of a mirror so they see what's happening, (3) two minutes of music as the timer. If resistance persists past 4 weeks, it's worth a pediatric dental consult.

patient_content_library.title: Aligners or braces? What I tell my patients in their 30s
patient_content_library.body: Aligners work for ~70% of cases — mild-to-moderate crowding, spacing, and minor rotations. Braces still win for severe rotations, deep bites, and skeletal corrections. The tell: if you can't keep aligners on for 22 hours/day, braces are the safer choice.

patient_content_library.body: A weekday thali for ₹149 covers your daily protein, two veggies, dal, rice, roti, and pickle/sweet. Buying these separately at any market in the neighbourhood: ₹240+. The only way restaurants make this work is high lunch volume — which means freshly-cooked, not standing in a chafer.

digest.title: Voluntary recall: Specific atorvastatin batches by manufacturer X

digest.actionable: Pull the batches; WhatsApp affected customers from your repeat-Rx list

patient_content_library.title: Generic vs branded medicines — what's actually different?
patient_content_library.body: The active molecule is identical and regulator-tested. Difference is in the binders, fillers, and brand markup. Generic is typically 40-60% cheaper. Two cases where brand may help: (1) very narrow-therapeutic-index drugs (some thyroid, blood thinners) where bioequivalence varies, (2) extended-release formulations. Otherwise, generic is the rational choice.

patient_content_library.title: Where to NOT store your medicines
patient_content_library.body: Three places: (1) bathroom — humidity wrecks tablet integrity. (2) car glove box — temperature swings degrade most molecules in a month. (3) refrigerator door — temperature varies too much; use the main shelf if cold storage is needed. The kitchen at room temperature, away from sunlight, is best.

patient_content_library.title: Summer first-aid: what every Indian household should have
patient_content_library.body: Five items: ORS sachets (heat-stroke is sudden), broad-spectrum sunscreen SPF 50+ (apply 30 min before sun), anti-fungal cream (sweat triggers infections), Vaseline (chafing relief), and a thermometer. Skip energy drinks marketed as 'summer drinks' — sugar load is counterproductive when dehydrated.

patient_content_library.title: First 30 days at the gym — what actually changes
patient_content_library.body: Week 1-2: nervous-system adaptation, lifts feel easier (not muscle gain — coordination). Week 3-4: small visible changes, sleep improves. Real strength/aesthetic changes start at week 8-12. Most people quit at week 3 expecting visible change. Trust the process.

patient_content_library.title: PT or solo? A 5-question test
patient_content_library.body: Solo if: you've trained before, you can read program structure, you don't get injured easily. PT if: you're new, recovering from injury, training for a specific event, or have inconsistent form. Hybrid (PT 1-2x/month + solo): the smartest middle path for most members.

patient_content_library.title: How much protein do you actually need?
patient_content_library.body: Sedentary adults: 0.8g/kg body weight. Active adults: 1.2-1.6g/kg. Strength training: 1.6-2.2g/kg. For a 70kg active person, that's 84-112g/day — roughly 4 fistful-portions of protein. Indian-veg diets often fall short; supplement with paneer, dal, tofu before adding powders.

patient_content_library.title: 3 things your teeth tell you about your heart
patient_content_library.body: Periodontal disease shares inflammation pathways with cardiovascular disease. Bleeding gums, persistent bad breath, and loose teeth in middle age are early signals. A 2024 meta-analysis of 18 studies found periodontal patients had 22% higher CVD events. The simplest action: scaling every 6 months reduces both gum and inflammation markers.

patient_content_library.title: Why your child resists brushing — and what to try this week
patient_content_library.body: Most resistance under age 7 is sensory, not behavioural. Try: (1) a softer brush than you think necessary, (2) brushing in front of a mirror so they see what's happening, (3) two minutes of music as the timer. If resistance persists past 4 weeks, it's worth a pediatric dental consult.

patient_content_library.title: Aligners or braces? What I tell my patients in their 30s
patient_content_library.body: Aligners work for ~70% of cases — mild-to-moderate crowding, spacing, and minor rotations. Braces still win for severe rotations, deep bites, and skeletal corrections. The tell: if you can't keep aligners on for 22 hours/day, braces are the safer choice.

patient_content_library.title: Is keratin safe? What to ask before booking
patient_content_library.body: Old-school keratin uses formaldehyde — safe in trained hands but not for pregnancy or asthma. Citric-acid alternatives exist now with similar hold. Two questions to ask any salon: (1) what's your activator? (2) is the room ventilated? If you don't get a clear answer, look elsewhere.

patient_content_library.title: Balayage vs highlights — which actually suits you?
patient_content_library.body: Highlights are uniform stripes; balayage is hand-painted, growing out softly with no visible regrowth line. Highlights cost less but need touch-ups every 8 weeks. Balayage holds 4-5 months. If you don't visit the salon often, balayage is the practical choice.

patient_content_library.title: Post-colour care — first 72 hours matter most
patient_content_library.body: Three rules: no shampoo for 48h (let cuticle close), use sulphate-free shampoo from day 3, and avoid hot water washes for the first month. Skip these and you'll lose 30% of the colour vibrance in the first wash.

patient_content_library.title: Three things to look for before ordering food online
patient_content_library.body: (1) Look at the latest review's date — if last review is 30+ days old, the kitchen may be off. (2) Filter for 4★+ reviews mentioning your specific dish. (3) Order at peak hours — fresh prep, not warmed-up morning batch. These three filter out 80% of bad-experience risk.

patient_content_library.title: Why a ₹149 thali is the best lunch deal in town
patient_content_library.body: A weekday thali for ₹149 covers your daily protein, two veggies, dal, rice, roti, and pickle/sweet. Buying these separately at any market in the neighbourhood: ₹240+. The only way restaurants make this work is high lunch volume — which means freshly-cooked, not standing in a chafer.

patient_content_library.title: Generic vs branded medicines — what's actually different?
patient_content_library.body: The active molecule is identical and regulator-tested. Difference is in the binders, fillers, and brand markup. Generic is typically 40-60% cheaper. Two cases where brand may help: (1) very narrow-therapeutic-index drugs (some thyroid, blood thinners) where bioequivalence varies, (2) extended-release formulations. Otherwise, generic is the rational choice.

patient_content_library.title: Where to NOT store your medicines
patient_content_library.body: Three places: (1) bathroom — humidity wrecks tablet integrity. (2) car glove box — temperature swings degrade most molecules in a month. (3) refrigerator door — temperature varies too much; use the main shelf if cold storage is needed. The kitchen at room temperature, away from sunlight, is best.

patient_content_library.title: Summer first-aid: what every Indian household should have
patient_content_library.body: Five items: ORS sachets (heat-stroke is sudden), broad-spectrum sunscreen SPF 50+ (apply 30 min before sun), anti-fungal cream (sweat triggers infections), Vaseline (chafing relief), and a thermometer. Skip energy drinks marketed as 'summer drinks' — sugar load is counterproductive when dehydrated.

patient_content_library.title: 3 things your teeth tell you about your heart
patient_content_library.body: Periodontal disease shares inflammation pathways with cardiovascular disease. Bleeding gums, persistent bad breath, and loose teeth in middle age are early signals. A 2024 meta-analysis of 18 studies found periodontal patients had 22% higher CVD events. The simplest action: scaling every 6 months reduces both gum and inflammation markers.

patient_content_library.title: Why your child resists brushing — and what to try this week
patient_content_library.body: Most resistance under age 7 is sensory, not behavioural. Try: (1) a softer brush than you think necessary, (2) brushing in front of a mirror so they see what's happening, (3) two minutes of music as the timer. If resistance persists past 4 weeks, it's worth a pediatric dental consult.

patient_content_library.title: Aligners or braces? What I tell my patients in their 30s
patient_content_library.body: Aligners work for ~70% of cases — mild-to-moderate crowding, spacing, and minor rotations. Braces still win for severe rotations, deep bites, and skeletal corrections. The tell: if you can't keep aligners on for 22 hours/day, braces are the safer choice.

patient_content_library.title: First 30 days at the gym — what actually changes
patient_content_library.body: Week 1-2: nervous-system adaptation, lifts feel easier (not muscle gain — coordination). Week 3-4: small visible changes, sleep improves. Real strength/aesthetic changes start at week 8-12. Most people quit at week 3 expecting visible change. Trust the process.

patient_content_library.title: PT or solo? A 5-question test
patient_content_library.body: Solo if: you've trained before, you can read program structure, you don't get injured easily. PT if: you're new, recovering from injury, training for a specific event, or have inconsistent form. Hybrid (PT 1-2x/month + solo): the smartest middle path for most members.

patient_content_library.title: How much protein do you actually need?
patient_content_library.body: Sedentary adults: 0.8g/kg body weight. Active adults: 1.2-1.6g/kg. Strength training: 1.6-2.2g/kg. For a 70kg active person, that's 84-112g/day — roughly 4 fistful-portions of protein. Indian-veg diets often fall short; supplement with paneer, dal, tofu before adding powders.

patient_content_library.title: First 30 days at the gym — what actually changes
patient_content_library.body: Week 1-2: nervous-system adaptation, lifts feel easier (not muscle gain — coordination). Week 3-4: small visible changes, sleep improves. Real strength/aesthetic changes start at week 8-12. Most people quit at week 3 expecting visible change. Trust the process.

patient_content_library.title: PT or solo? A 5-question test
patient_content_library.body: Solo if: you've trained before, you can read program structure, you don't get injured easily. PT if: you're new, recovering from injury, training for a specific event, or have inconsistent form. Hybrid (PT 1-2x/month + solo): the smartest middle path for most members.

patient_content_library.title: How much protein do you actually need?
patient_content_library.body: Sedentary adults: 0.8g/kg body weight. Active adults: 1.2-1.6g/kg. Strength training: 1.6-2.2g/kg. For a 70kg active person, that's 84-112g/day — roughly 4 fistful-portions of protein. Indian-veg diets often fall short; supplement with paneer, dal, tofu before adding powders.

patient_content_library.title: Is keratin safe? What to ask before booking
patient_content_library.body: Old-school keratin uses formaldehyde — safe in trained hands but not for pregnancy or asthma. Citric-acid alternatives exist now with similar hold. Two questions to ask any salon: (1) what's your activator? (2) is the room ventilated? If you don't get a clear answer, look elsewhere.

patient_content_library.title: Balayage vs highlights — which actually suits you?
patient_content_library.body: Highlights are uniform stripes; balayage is hand-painted, growing out softly with no visible regrowth line. Highlights cost less but need touch-ups every 8 weeks. Balayage holds 4-5 months. If you don't visit the salon often, balayage is the practical choice.

patient_content_library.title: Post-colour care — first 72 hours matter most
patient_content_library.body: Three rules: no shampoo for 48h (let cuticle close), use sulphate-free shampoo from day 3, and avoid hot water washes for the first month. Skip these and you'll lose 30% of the colour vibrance in the first wash.

patient_content_library.title: Generic vs branded medicines — what's actually different?
patient_content_library.body: The active molecule is identical and regulator-tested. Difference is in the binders, fillers, and brand markup. Generic is typically 40-60% cheaper. Two cases where brand may help: (1) very narrow-therapeutic-index drugs (some thyroid, blood thinners) where bioequivalence varies, (2) extended-release formulations. Otherwise, generic is the rational choice.

patient_content_library.title: Where to NOT store your medicines
patient_content_library.body: Three places: (1) bathroom — humidity wrecks tablet integrity. (2) car glove box — temperature swings degrade most molecules in a month. (3) refrigerator door — temperature varies too much; use the main shelf if cold storage is needed. The kitchen at room temperature, away from sunlight, is best.

patient_content_library.title: Summer first-aid: what every Indian household should have
patient_content_library.body: Five items: ORS sachets (heat-stroke is sudden), broad-spectrum sunscreen SPF 50+ (apply 30 min before sun), anti-fungal cream (sweat triggers infections), Vaseline (chafing relief), and a thermometer. Skip energy drinks marketed as 'summer drinks' — sugar load is counterproductive when dehydrated.

patient_content_library.title: 3 things your teeth tell you about your heart
patient_content_library.body: Periodontal disease shares inflammation pathways with cardiovascular disease. Bleeding gums, persistent bad breath, and loose teeth in middle age are early signals. A 2024 meta-analysis of 18 studies found periodontal patients had 22% higher CVD events. The simplest action: scaling every 6 months reduces both gum and inflammation markers.

patient_content_library.title: Why your child resists brushing — and what to try this week
patient_content_library.body: Most resistance under age 7 is sensory, not behavioural. Try: (1) a softer brush than you think necessary, (2) brushing in front of a mirror so they see what's happening, (3) two minutes of music as the timer. If resistance persists past 4 weeks, it's worth a pediatric dental consult.

patient_content_library.title: Aligners or braces? What I tell my patients in their 30s
patient_content_library.body: Aligners work for ~70% of cases — mild-to-moderate crowding, spacing, and minor rotations. Braces still win for severe rotations, deep bites, and skeletal corrections. The tell: if you can't keep aligners on for 22 hours/day, braces are the safer choice.

digest.title: ICMR creatine supplementation safety bulletin — adolescent guidance

digest.title: Schedule density study — peak slots underutilized in mornings

digest.actionable: Consider one new 7am class — yoga or HIIT pulls best in that slot

patient_content_library.title: First 30 days at the gym — what actually changes
patient_content_library.body: Week 1-2: nervous-system adaptation, lifts feel easier (not muscle gain — coordination). Week 3-4: small visible changes, sleep improves. Real strength/aesthetic changes start at week 8-12. Most people quit at week 3 expecting visible change. Trust the process.

patient_content_library.title: PT or solo? A 5-question test
patient_content_library.body: Solo if: you've trained before, you can read program structure, you don't get injured easily. PT if: you're new, recovering from injury, training for a specific event, or have inconsistent form. Hybrid (PT 1-2x/month + solo): the smartest middle path for most members.

patient_content_library.title: How much protein do you actually need?
patient_content_library.body: Sedentary adults: 0.8g/kg body weight. Active adults: 1.2-1.6g/kg. Strength training: 1.6-2.2g/kg. For a 70kg active person, that's 84-112g/day — roughly 4 fistful-portions of protein. Indian-veg diets often fall short; supplement with paneer, dal, tofu before adding powders.

patient_content_library.title: Generic vs branded medicines — what's actually different?
patient_content_library.body: The active molecule is identical and regulator-tested. Difference is in the binders, fillers, and brand markup. Generic is typically 40-60% cheaper. Two cases where brand may help: (1) very narrow-therapeutic-index drugs (some thyroid, blood thinners) where bioequivalence varies, (2) extended-release formulations. Otherwise, generic is the rational choice.

patient_content_library.title: Where to NOT store your medicines
patient_content_library.body: Three places: (1) bathroom — humidity wrecks tablet integrity. (2) car glove box — temperature swings degrade most molecules in a month. (3) refrigerator door — temperature varies too much; use the main shelf if cold storage is needed. The kitchen at room temperature, away from sunlight, is best.

patient_content_library.title: Summer first-aid: what every Indian household should have
patient_content_library.body: Five items: ORS sachets (heat-stroke is sudden), broad-spectrum sunscreen SPF 50+ (apply 30 min before sun), anti-fungal cream (sweat triggers infections), Vaseline (chafing relief), and a thermometer. Skip energy drinks marketed as 'summer drinks' — sugar load is counterproductive when dehydrated.

patient_content_library.title: Is keratin safe? What to ask before booking
patient_content_library.body: Old-school keratin uses formaldehyde — safe in trained hands but not for pregnancy or asthma. Citric-acid alternatives exist now with similar hold. Two questions to ask any salon: (1) what's your activator? (2) is the room ventilated? If you don't get a clear answer, look elsewhere.

patient_content_library.title: Balayage vs highlights — which actually suits you?
patient_content_library.body: Highlights are uniform stripes; balayage is hand-painted, growing out softly with no visible regrowth line. Highlights cost less but need touch-ups every 8 weeks. Balayage holds 4-5 months. If you don't visit the salon often, balayage is the practical choice.

patient_content_library.title: Post-colour care — first 72 hours matter most
patient_content_library.body: Three rules: no shampoo for 48h (let cuticle close), use sulphate-free shampoo from day 3, and avoid hot water washes for the first month. Skip these and you'll lose 30% of the colour vibrance in the first wash.

patient_content_library.title: Three things to look for before ordering food online
patient_content_library.body: (1) Look at the latest review's date — if last review is 30+ days old, the kitchen may be off. (2) Filter for 4★+ reviews mentioning your specific dish. (3) Order at peak hours — fresh prep, not warmed-up morning batch. These three filter out 80% of bad-experience risk.

patient_content_library.title: Why a ₹149 thali is the best lunch deal in town
patient_content_library.body: A weekday thali for ₹149 covers your daily protein, two veggies, dal, rice, roti, and pickle/sweet. Buying these separately at any market in the neighbourhood: ₹240+. The only way restaurants make this work is high lunch volume — which means freshly-cooked, not standing in a chafer.

patient_content_library.title: 3 things your teeth tell you about your heart
patient_content_library.body: Periodontal disease shares inflammation pathways with cardiovascular disease. Bleeding gums, persistent bad breath, and loose teeth in middle age are early signals. A 2024 meta-analysis of 18 studies found periodontal patients had 22% higher CVD events. The simplest action: scaling every 6 months reduces both gum and inflammation markers.

patient_content_library.title: Why your child resists brushing — and what to try this week
patient_content_library.body: Most resistance under age 7 is sensory, not behavioural. Try: (1) a softer brush than you think necessary, (2) brushing in front of a mirror so they see what's happening, (3) two minutes of music as the timer. If resistance persists past 4 weeks, it's worth a pediatric dental consult.

patient_content_library.title: Aligners or braces? What I tell my patients in their 30s
patient_content_library.body: Aligners work for ~70% of cases — mild-to-moderate crowding, spacing, and minor rotations. Braces still win for severe rotations, deep bites, and skeletal corrections. The tell: if you can't keep aligners on for 22 hours/day, braces are the safer choice.

patient_content_library.title: First 30 days at the gym — what actually changes
patient_content_library.body: Week 1-2: nervous-system adaptation, lifts feel easier (not muscle gain — coordination). Week 3-4: small visible changes, sleep improves. Real strength/aesthetic changes start at week 8-12. Most people quit at week 3 expecting visible change. Trust the process.

patient_content_library.title: PT or solo? A 5-question test
patient_content_library.body: Solo if: you've trained before, you can read program structure, you don't get injured easily. PT if: you're new, recovering from injury, training for a specific event, or have inconsistent form. Hybrid (PT 1-2x/month + solo): the smartest middle path for most members.

patient_content_library.title: How much protein do you actually need?
patient_content_library.body: Sedentary adults: 0.8g/kg body weight. Active adults: 1.2-1.6g/kg. Strength training: 1.6-2.2g/kg. For a 70kg active person, that's 84-112g/day — roughly 4 fistful-portions of protein. Indian-veg diets often fall short; supplement with paneer, dal, tofu before adding powders.

patient_content_library.title: Generic vs branded medicines — what's actually different?
patient_content_library.body: The active molecule is identical and regulator-tested. Difference is in the binders, fillers, and brand markup. Generic is typically 40-60% cheaper. Two cases where brand may help: (1) very narrow-therapeutic-index drugs (some thyroid, blood thinners) where bioequivalence varies, (2) extended-release formulations. Otherwise, generic is the rational choice.

patient_content_library.title: Where to NOT store your medicines
patient_content_library.body: Three places: (1) bathroom — humidity wrecks tablet integrity. (2) car glove box — temperature swings degrade most molecules in a month. (3) refrigerator door — temperature varies too much; use the main shelf if cold storage is needed. The kitchen at room temperature, away from sunlight, is best.

patient_content_library.title: Summer first-aid: what every Indian household should have
patient_content_library.body: Five items: ORS sachets (heat-stroke is sudden), broad-spectrum sunscreen SPF 50+ (apply 30 min before sun), anti-fungal cream (sweat triggers infections), Vaseline (chafing relief), and a thermometer. Skip energy drinks marketed as 'summer drinks' — sugar load is counterproductive when dehydrated.

patient_content_library.title: Generic vs branded medicines — what's actually different?
patient_content_library.body: The active molecule is identical and regulator-tested. Difference is in the binders, fillers, and brand markup. Generic is typically 40-60% cheaper. Two cases where brand may help: (1) very narrow-therapeutic-index drugs (some thyroid, blood thinners) where bioequivalence varies, (2) extended-release formulations. Otherwise, generic is the rational choice.

patient_content_library.title: Where to NOT store your medicines
patient_content_library.body: Three places: (1) bathroom — humidity wrecks tablet integrity. (2) car glove box — temperature swings degrade most molecules in a month. (3) refrigerator door — temperature varies too much; use the main shelf if cold storage is needed. The kitchen at room temperature, away from sunlight, is best.

patient_content_library.title: Summer first-aid: what every Indian household should have
patient_content_library.body: Five items: ORS sachets (heat-stroke is sudden), broad-spectrum sunscreen SPF 50+ (apply 30 min before sun), anti-fungal cream (sweat triggers infections), Vaseline (chafing relief), and a thermometer. Skip energy drinks marketed as 'summer drinks' — sugar load is counterproductive when dehydrated.

patient_content_library.title: Is keratin safe? What to ask before booking
patient_content_library.body: Old-school keratin uses formaldehyde — safe in trained hands but not for pregnancy or asthma. Citric-acid alternatives exist now with similar hold. Two questions to ask any salon: (1) what's your activator? (2) is the room ventilated? If you don't get a clear answer, look elsewhere.

patient_content_library.title: Balayage vs highlights — which actually suits you?
patient_content_library.body: Highlights are uniform stripes; balayage is hand-painted, growing out softly with no visible regrowth line. Highlights cost less but need touch-ups every 8 weeks. Balayage holds 4-5 months. If you don't visit the salon often, balayage is the practical choice.

patient_content_library.title: Post-colour care — first 72 hours matter most
patient_content_library.body: Three rules: no shampoo for 48h (let cuticle close), use sulphate-free shampoo from day 3, and avoid hot water washes for the first month. Skip these and you'll lose 30% of the colour vibrance in the first wash.

patient_content_library.title: First 30 days at the gym — what actually changes
patient_content_library.body: Week 1-2: nervous-system adaptation, lifts feel easier (not muscle gain — coordination). Week 3-4: small visible changes, sleep improves. Real strength/aesthetic changes start at week 8-12. Most people quit at week 3 expecting visible change. Trust the process.

patient_content_library.title: PT or solo? A 5-question test
patient_content_library.body: Solo if: you've trained before, you can read program structure, you don't get injured easily. PT if: you're new, recovering from injury, training for a specific event, or have inconsistent form. Hybrid (PT 1-2x/month + solo): the smartest middle path for most members.

patient_content_library.title: How much protein do you actually need?
patient_content_library.body: Sedentary adults: 0.8g/kg body weight. Active adults: 1.2-1.6g/kg. Strength training: 1.6-2.2g/kg. For a 70kg active person, that's 84-112g/day — roughly 4 fistful-portions of protein. Indian-veg diets often fall short; supplement with paneer, dal, tofu before adding powders.

The only way restaurants make this work is high lunch volume — which means freshly-cooked, not standing in a chafer.
